mnemonics.title: Flynn's Taxonomy Rememberer
mnemonics.content: SISD: Single = One processor, SIMD: Single instruction on Multiple Data = vector/GPU, MISD: Multiple Instructions on Single Data = systolic pipeline, MIMD: Multiple Instructions on Multiple Data = multi-core cluster. Think: 'Some Instruction Single Data' - most common for parallel.

mnemonics.title: UMA vs NUMA Memory
mnemonics.content: UMA: Uniform Memory Access - shared bus, all equal, like a conference room where everyone is equidistant from the center. NUMA: Non-Uniform - local is fast, remote is slow, like a campus where buildings have local resources but travel takes time.

mnemonics.title: Amdahl's Law Formula
mnemonics.content: Speedup S = 1/(1-P) where P = parallel portion. If P=0.9 (90% parallel), S ≤ 10x. If P=0.99, S ≤ 100x. The serial 1-P portion limits everything - like a chain's strength is limited by its weakest link.

mnemonics.title: SPMD vs MPMD
mnemonics.content: SPMD: Same Program Different Data - all workers run identical code on different pieces. MPMD: Multiple Programs Different Data - different processes run different programs, like a factory assembly line.

acronyms.usage: Traditional sequential computers - one instruction processes one data item at a time
Vector processors, GPUs - same operation on multiple data elements simultaneously
Multi-core CPUs, distributed clusters - independent processors with independent data streams
Rare - systolic arrays, fault-tolerant systems - same data through multiple processors
Common execution model - same program runs on all processes with different data subsets
Different programs on different processors - used in heterogeneous computing
Shared memory where all processors have equal access time to any memory location
Memory access time depends on processor-memory distance; local memory is faster
Cache hierarchy - L1 fastest/smallest, L3 slowest/largest, typically shared across cores

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 2x2 matrix: Rows = Instruction (Single/Multiple), Columns = Data (Single/Multiple). Fill SISD (sequential), SIMD (vector/GPU), MIMD (multi-core), MISD (systolic). This forms a mental map of all four categories.
Imagine a multi-socket motherboard: draw circles for CPU sockets, connect them with a fast interconnect, place memory chips near each socket. Show that local paths are shorter than remote paths.

Draw a bar showing total work as 100%. Mark the serial portion (1-P) as irreducible. Show parallel portion P being divided among processors, but the serial part never shrinks - this is the bottleneck.
Draw horizontal rows of nodes (2^n), with vertical connections showing paths. Each column represents one routing stage. Show how any input reaches any output in exactly n hops - this is the diameter.

analogies.analogy: Think of a shared whiteboard in an office - all workers (processors) can read/write to it directly. They need to coordinate (locks) so they don't overwrite each other's work. Fast but requires synchronization.
Think of separate offices with intercoms - each worker has private documents (local memory) and must send messages (intercom calls) to share information. Slower but scales to many processors.

Imagine washing dishes: the serial part is scraping (can't parallelize), the parallel part is scrubbing plates (many people can do simultaneously). Even with 100 scrubbers, you still need time for scraping - that's your speedup limit.

Imagine multiple people editing the same Google Doc (cache copies). Snooping is like everyone watching the document for changes. Directory-based is like a manager (directory) who tracks who's editing what and notifies others of changes.
